nativeRange.habitat: Tropical and subtropical streamside microhabitats; grows attached to wood, rocks, and tree roots in humid riparian zones, consistent with other Taxiphyllum mosses

decorPreferences: driftwood and branching wood (vertical flame-shaped fronds showcase well on wood)
stainless or plastic mesh for moss walls
tied with thread/fishing line or attached with cyanoacrylate gel until rhizoids anchor
gentle to moderate flow keeps fronds free of debris and accentuates the upward growth

summary: Flame Moss is an Asian aquarium moss prized for its distinctive upward-spiraling, flame-shaped fronds — a striking departure from the sprawling habit of Java Moss. Generally advised to attach to driftwood or rock, it tolerates a wide range of light and water conditions and suits tanks of most sizes.
careNotes:
Flame Moss (Taxiphyllum sp. 'Flame') is a trade-designated Asian moss in the family Hypnaceae, closely related to Java Moss ([fw-plant-033]) and Christmas Moss ([fw-plant-114]). Its most recognisable trait is its UPWARD, SPIRALING, FLAME-SHAPED growth — fronds twist gently as they ascend, giving the overall appearance of a deep green campfire. The species is widely regarded as an undescribed Taxiphyllum that has circulated in the aquarium trade without a formal scientific description.

Attachment: Flame Moss is an epiphyte, meaning it draws nutrients from the water rather than from soil and anchors itself to surfaces rather than rooting into substrate. Tie or glue it to driftwood, porous rock, lava rock, or plastic or stainless mesh — DO NOT bury it in substrate. Rhizoids (tiny root-like anchoring structures) will bond the moss to the surface over several weeks.

Lighting: the moss is generally tolerant of low to medium light levels. It survives in low-light setups, but moderate light tends to encourage denser, fuller, and more strongly upright growth.

CO2: supplemental CO2 is optional. The moss grows without it but tends to grow faster and more densely with CO2 injection.

Water: temperature tolerance is broadly tropical, around 20–28 °C (68–82 °F). pH tolerance is wide, with 6.0–7.5 being a commonly recommended aquarium target.

Fertilization: demand is low. The moss absorbs nutrients directly from the water column and does not require a nutrient-rich substrate. Weekly liquid fertilization has been reported to encourage fuller growth, but it is not essential.

Shrimp and fry refuge: the upright fronds form dense microhabitat well suited to newly hatched fry and dwarf-shrimp larvae, which graze the biofilm and microfauna that colonise the moss.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only in aquarium conditions: cut or rip off any healthy portion of the mat and re-attach it to a new surface — every fragment regrows. There is no need to wait for any reproductive cycle. True sexual reproduction (spore production from a sporophyte) requires emersed growth and is essentially never observed in submerged aquarium tanks.

primary.name: Buce Plant — Flame Moss
primary.notes: Primary product page. Used for: scientific name (Taxiphyllum 'Flame'), care level (easy), pH 6–8, lighting (low to medium), CO2 not necessary but recommended, propagation by 'cut and rip', epiphyte placement (can be attached to hardscape or left free-floating), and warnings against drastic parameter changes (which can cause melt and rot). Notes that growth and coloration improve with CO2 injection.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Taxiphyllum sp. 'Flame Moss' Tissue Culture
additional.notes: Secondary cross-verification source. Used for: scientific name confirmation (Taxiphyllum sp. 'Flame Moss'), trade synonym 'Asian Flame Moss', height range 5–15 cm, slow horizontal spread, vertical undulating growth pattern (described as 'a deep green campfire'), easy care level, and propagation by dividing portions. Tissue-culture product, so no fertilizer/parameter specifics on the page itself.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Taxiphyllum
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority. Used for: confirmation of family Hypnaceae and order Hypnales, genus description as 'mat-forming' with 'glossy and creeping' stems, and genus distribution covering Asia (India, Japan, Philippines among others). Wikipedia does not have a dedicated entry for the 'Flame' trade designation — only Java Moss (T. barbieri) is documented as an economically important aquarium species.

additional.name: Aquariumplants.com — Flame Moss
additional.notes: Third cross-verification source for water parameters. Used for: temperature 20–28 °C (68–82 °F), pH 6.0–7.5, low to moderate lighting, CO2 optional but accelerates growth, slow to moderate growth rate, dense low clump form (2–5 cm in their reading), foreground/midground placement. Confirms the species is 'often listed as Taxiphyllum sp.' with exact species uncertain — corroborating the undescribed-trade-species status.

propagation: cut and rip — divide and re-attach to new surfaces
